content: I'll start by reading my instructions and the target chapter.

1	---
2	name: blueprint-writer
3	description: Update one blueprint chapter to reflect strategy changes, fill missing definitions or theorems, or align prose with the current Lean structure. Plan-agent-dispatched; one writer per chapter. May spawn reference-retriever mid-session when drafting reveals a missing source.
4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: false
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch one writer per chapter that the most recent blueprint
10	    review (the blueprint-review subagent in your catalog, when
11	    present) flagged as incomplete, lacking proof detail, or missing
12	    multi-route coverage.
13	  - Each directive must be precise: strategy context (the slice that
14	    matters for this chapter), required definitions/theorems (with
15	    enough mathematical detail to formalize), references, and
16	    explicit out-of-scope items. Writers do NOT speculate beyond
17	    what the directive lists.
18	  - **NEVER instruct the writer to add ``\leanok`` or ``\mathlibok``
19	    markers** (no "after writing the block, add `\leanok`"; no
20	    "verification: confirm `\leanok` is present"). The writer's
21	    descriptor forbids it — ``\leanok`` is managed by the
22	    deterministic ``sync_leanok`` phase, ``\mathlibok`` by the review
23	    agent. A directive that asks the writer to add markers puts it in
24	    a rule conflict; the writer must obey its descriptor, so the
25	    "verification" instruction will appear to fail until sync_leanok
26	    runs. Leave marker concerns out of the writer's directive entirely.
27	  - **Authorize the retriever in the writer's --write-domain.** If
28	    the chapter might need fresh source material, dispatch the
29	    writer with TWO globs:
30	      --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter>.tex'
31	      --write-domain 'references/**'
32	    The writer itself only edits its assigned chapter (its prompt
33	    body enforces that), but the second glob authorizes a child
34	    reference-retriever if the writer discovers it needs one. Omit
35	    `references/**` only when you are confident the writer will not
36	    need new sources (e.g. purely cleanup edits).
37	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and one route's chapters
38	    are missing, dispatch a writer per route to bring all routes to
39	    parity before any prover work begins. Do not push provers onto a
40	    route whose blueprint coverage is incomplete.
41	  - After a significant writer round, re-run the blueprint review
42	    in the same iteration (the relevant entry in your catalog) to
43	    confirm the updated blueprint is now sufficient — do not assume
44	    the writer fixed everything.
45	  - If a writer's report contains entries under "Strategy-modifying
46	    findings", STOP and update STRATEGY.md before any further Lean
47	    work this iter. The writer is telling you the prose surfaced a
48	    strategy-level issue.
49	  - If a writer's report includes child reference-retriever dispatches,
50	    skim the new `references/<slug>.md` files before using the
51	    writer's output — the writer relied on them, so should you.
52	---
53	
54	# Blueprint Writer
55	
56	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
57	
58	## Your Job
59	
60	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
61	
62	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
63	
64	## Directive Format
65	
66	```markdown
67	# Blueprint Writer Directive
68	
69	## Slug
70	<slug>
71	
72	## Target chapter
73	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
74	
75	## Strategy context
76	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
77	
78	## Required content
79	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
80	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
81	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
82	- ...
83	
84	## Out of scope
85	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
86	
87	## References
88	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
89	- ...
90	
91	## Expected outcome
92	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
93	```
94	
95	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
96	
97	## Chapter format
98	
99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
107	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
108	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
109	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
110	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
111	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
112	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
113	\end{theorem}
114	
115	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
116	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
117	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
118	\begin{proof}
119	  \uses{thm:another_result}
120	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
121	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
122	\end{proof}
123	```
124	
125	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
126	
127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139	
140	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
147	
148	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
149	
150	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
151	
152	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
153	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
154	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
155	
156	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
157	
158	## Rules
159	
160	### What you CAN do
161	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
162	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
163	- Add `\uses{...}` cross-references.
164	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
165	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
166	
167	### What you MUST do
168	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
169	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
170	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
171	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
172	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
173	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
174	
175	### What you MUST NOT do
176	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` or `\mathlibok` markers.** Those are managed by the `sync_leanok` phase + the review agent — never by you.
177	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
178	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
179	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
180	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
181	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
182	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
183	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
184	
185	## Reading references
186	
187	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
188	
189	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
190	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
191	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
192	
193	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
194	
195	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
196	
197	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
198	
199	Conditions for dispatch:
200	
201	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
202	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
203	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
204	
205	Dispatch (Bash, foreground, in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
206	
207	```
208	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
209	  --name reference-retriever \
210	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
211	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
212	  --write-domain 'references/**'
213	```
214	
215	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
216	
217	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
218	
219	## Workflow
220	
221	1. Read your directive completely.
222	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
223	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
224	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
225	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
226	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
227	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
228	8. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
229	
230	## Logging
231	
232	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-writer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
233	
234	```markdown
235	# Blueprint Writer Report
236	
237	## Slug
238	<slug>
239	
240	## Status
241	<COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE>
242	<If INCOMPLETE: which required items could not be written and why.>
243	
244	## Target chapter
245	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
246	
247	## Changes Made
248	- **Added definition** `\definition`/`\label{def:foo}`/`\lean{Foo.bar}` — <one line on what it captures>
249	- **Added theorem** `\theorem`/`\label{thm:foo}` — <one line on statement>
250	  - Proof sketch added: <Y/N + brief shape>
251	- **Revised** `<existing label>` — <one line on what changed>
252	- ...
253	
254	## Cross-references introduced
255	- `\uses{thm:bar}` added in proof of `\thm:foo` — verify `thm:bar` exists in <chapter or this same one>
256	- ...
257	
258	## References consulted
259	<every local file under `references/` you OPENED and READ this session, with a one-line note on what you took from each. The plan agent and the reviewer use this list to verify that every `% SOURCE:` parenthetical points at a file you actually read. Omit this section only when you wrote zero citation blocks (Archon-original chapter).>
260	- `references/hartshorne-III-5.md` — verbatim quote for `\thm:smooth_criterion` (statement + proof).
261	- `references/stacks-tag-01V4.md` — verbatim quote for `\def:etale_morphism`.
262	
263	## Macros needed (if any)
264	- `\foo{...}` — used in <where>; needs definition in `macros/common.tex`. NOT added by me (out of write-domain).
265	
266	## Reference-retriever dispatches (if any)
267	- slug `<child-slug>`: requested `<source>`. Status: COMPLETE / NOT_FOUND / PARTIAL. Summary at `references/<slug>.md`.
268	- ...
269	
270	## Notes for Plan Agent
271	- <any inconsistency I noticed in sibling chapters that the directive didn't cover>
272	- <any structural concern: e.g. "the chapter is now 800 lines, consider splitting">
273	- <any reference I had to guess at because the directive's reference section was incomplete>
274	
275	## Strategy-modifying findings
276	<populate this section ONLY if writing the chapter surfaced a need to
277	change the project's strategy itself — not just the chapter content.
278	Examples:
279	- A definition you were asked to write turns out to require a property
280	  that the strategy assumes is automatic but is not.
281	- A theorem you sketched is provable as stated, but its consequence
282	  used elsewhere in STRATEGY.md does not follow from it.
283	- A reference cited as authoritative actually requires a different
284	  setup than the strategy assumes.
285	
286	When this section is non-empty, the plan agent must update STRATEGY.md
287	before any further Lean work this iteration. Do NOT silently adapt the
288	chapter to paper over a strategy-level issue — surface it here.>
289	```
290	
291	## Return Value
292	
293	Your final assistant message:
294	
295	- One line: `<slug>: COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE — <one-sentence outcome>`
296	- The path to your full report.
297	
298	Keep the inline return short. The plan agent reads the full report.
299

This is a prose reconciliation task — no external citations needed (the flagged passages are project-internal strategy framing). I've identified the three stale passages. Let me make minimal edits to each.

The first stale block is in the proof of `thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp` (line 82):

input.old_string: In genus $0$, the same statement reduces to the rigidity theorem for $C \to A$ established \emph{directly over $k$} --- the named declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, whose signature is $k$-agnostic per the iter-127 over-k commitment (\texttt{analogies/cotangent-vanishing-pile-over-k.md}) and which is built out of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii) over an arbitrary base field with no algebraic-closure, perfect-base, or Galois-descent hypothesis. See Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, sub-cases C.1--C.3, in particular the seven-step expansion C.2.a--C.2.g, where sub-step C.2.f (Galois descent of morphism equality back to $k$) is \textbf{DROPPED} under the iter-127 commitment: no base-change-and-descent step appears anywhere in the M2 critical path.
input.new_string: In genus $0$, the same statement reduces to the rigidity theorem for $C \to A$ --- the named declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}. This rigidity result is established \emph{over the algebraic closure $\bar k$}: its signature carries an $\mathrm{IsAlgClosed}\,\bar k$ hypothesis, and it is built out of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii) over the algebraically closed base. The resulting equality of morphisms is then descended back to the original base field $k$ by faithfully-flat descent: because $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$ is faithfully flat, two $k$-morphisms that agree after base change to $\bar k$ already agree over $k$ (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}). See Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, sub-cases C.1--C.3, in particular the seven-step expansion C.2.a--C.2.g, where sub-step C.2.f (faithfully-flat descent of morphism equality from $\bar k$ back to $k$) is the descent step carrying the algebraically-closed-base rigidity conclusion down to the original base field; a base-change-to-$\bar k$ step (establishing rigidity) followed by this descent step is on the M2 critical path.

# Blueprint Writer Report

## Slug
abeljacobi-descent

## Status
COMPLETE

## Target chapter
blueprint/src/chapters/AbelJacobi.tex

## Changes Made
- **Revised** proof of `thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp` (genus-$0$ paragraph) —
  replaced the stale "rigidity established directly over $k$ / signature is
  $k$-agnostic / no algebraic-closure hypothesis / sub-step C.2.f DROPPED"
  framing. Now states that `\cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}` is proved over the
  algebraic closure $\bar k$ (signature carries an $\mathrm{IsAlgClosed}\,\bar k$
  hypothesis, built from cotangent-vanishing pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii) over the
  algebraically closed base), and that the morphism equality is descended to $k$
  by faithfully-flat descent ($\Spec\bar k \to \Spec k$ faithfully flat;
  `AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi_of_flat_of_surjective`). Recast sub-step C.2.f as
  the *reinstated* descent step (no longer DROPPED) and noted the
  base-change-then-descent step is on the M2 critical path.
- **Revised** §"Implementation route", route-list sentence — "established
  directly over $k$ … sub-step C.2.f DROPPED" → "proved over $\bar k$ and
  descended to $k$ … which is precisely sub-step C.2.f of §C.2", with the
  $\mathrm{IsAlgClosed}\,\bar k$ hypothesis noted.
- **Revised** §"Implementation route", classical-proof sentence — "established
  directly over $k$ … no base-change to $\bar k$ and no Galois descent enter" →
  "proved over $\bar k$ … and descended to $k$ by faithfully-flat descent of
  morphism equality (`AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi_of_flat_of_surjective`)".

## Cross-references introduced
- None. `\cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}`, `\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}`, and
  `\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}` were all already present; no new labels.

## References consulted
- None. These passages are project-internal strategy framing (Archon-original),
  not external-source-derived, so no `% SOURCE:` citation blocks apply. No
  reference-retriever dispatched.

## Macros needed (if any)
- None. Used plain `$\mathrm{IsAlgClosed}\,\bar k$` and `\texttt{...}` with
  escaped underscores; no new macro required.

## Notes for Plan Agent
- **Cross-chapter consistency (flag, not fixed):** the same stale "over-k
  commitment / sub-step C.2.f DROPPED / $k$-agnostic rigidity" framing very
  likely also appears in `blueprint/src/chapters/Jacobian.tex` (the
  `thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness` proof, sub-cases C.1--C.3 and the C.2.a--C.2.g
  expansion that this chapter cross-references) and in
  `blueprint/src/chapters/RigidityKbar.tex` (the `thm:rigidity_over_kbar`
  statement block, whose signature must now show `[IsAlgClosed kbar]`). I did
  not touch those (out of scope). Recommend dispatching writers for those two
  chapters to keep the §C.2 narrative and the rigidity statement consistent with
  the descent pivot — otherwise this chapter's "sub-step C.2.f is the descent
  step" now disagrees with a Jacobian.tex that may still call it DROPPED.
- The analogy note `analogies/cotangent-vanishing-pile-over-k.md` was cited by
  the old prose as the authority for the over-k commitment; that citation is now
  removed from this chapter. If that file still asserts the over-k framing it is
  stale background — worth a glance.

## Strategy-modifying findings
- None. This was a consistency/prose reconciliation reflecting an
  already-decided strategy pivot; no new strategy-level issue surfaced.
